turns.content: Saturday morning, coffee in hand, and I am trying to plan a hike for tomorrow. I could use some recommendations for trails in the Portland area that are worthwhile this time of year.

Great time to get out there — late March in the Portland area means the waterfalls are running strong and the wildflowers are just starting. What kind of hike are you looking for? Distance, elevation gain, scenery preferences? And are you thinking Columbia River Gorge, the coast range, or something closer in?

I am thinking moderate difficulty. Somewhere in the six to ten mile range. I have done most of the well-known Gorge trails multiple times over the years, so I would love something a little less obvious. I would rather not fight for parking at Multnomah Falls with a few hundred tourists.

Completely understandable. A few less-trafficked options that should be excellent right now:

1. **Elk Mountain via Elk-Kings Traverse** (Tillamook State Forest) — About 10 miles if you do the full traverse, with a solid 2,000+ feet of elevation gain. It's a real workout through old-growth forest and you'll likely have the trail mostly to yourself. You'd need a car shuttle or an out-and-back.

2. **Hardy Ridge** (near Bonneville) — Around 7 miles round trip with views across the Gorge that rival Angel's Rest but with a fraction of the foot traffic. There are some nice meadow sections that might be greening up.

3. **Salmon River Trail** (near Welches) — A flatter option, about 8 miles out and back along the only wild and scenic river in the lower 48 that's designated from its headwaters. Old growth, moss everywhere, very peaceful.

Any of those sound appealing?

Hardy Ridge is interesting. I have done the trails around Bonneville a handful of times but I do not think I have ever taken that particular route. You mentioned meadow sections — is the upper portion exposed? I am not particularly interested in being battered by wind for three hours.

There is some exposure on the upper ridgeline, yes. The first half of the climb is well-sheltered through forest, but once you hit the meadows near the top, you're on an open ridge with panoramic views — which also means you're at the mercy of whatever the wind is doing. March in the Gorge can be unpredictable on that front. You could check the forecast specifically for Beacon Rock or Bonneville Dam to get a sense of wind conditions. If it's calm, the ridge is spectacular. If it's a typical Gorge wind day, you might prefer the Salmon River Trail, which is almost entirely sheltered in dense forest.

That is sensible advice. I will check the forecast tomorrow morning and make a game-time decision. There is something appealing about the Salmon River option regardless. Old growth along a river sounds like the kind of quiet I need right now. The week was long.

The Salmon River Trail is excellent for exactly that kind of restorative quiet. It's the kind of hike where the loudest thing you hear is the river. A few practical notes: the trailhead is off Highway 26 near the Zigzag Ranger Station, parking is usually not an issue, and the trail is well-maintained. The first couple of miles are the most scenic, so even if you don't feel like doing the full eight, you'll get the best of it early. Bring layers — it stays cool under that canopy even when Portland is warming up.

Good to know about the layers. I have made the mistake before of dressing for the temperature in town and then spending four hours shivering under a forest canopy. On a related note, is there anything along the Salmon River Trail in terms of creek crossings that might be tricky this time of year? Late March with snowmelt can turn a minor crossing into something more eventful.

Good question. There are a few creek crossings along the trail, mostly on simple log bridges or stepping stones. In late March with active snowmelt, the creek levels will be higher than in summer, but the crossings on the first four miles are generally manageable — nothing that requires fording. Further along the trail past the four-mile mark, there's at least one crossing that can get a bit more adventurous in high water. If you're keeping it to the scenic first half, you should be fine. Trekking poles help with balance on any wet rocks if you have them.

I do have trekking poles, though I confess I resisted them for years on philosophical grounds. They felt like an admission of something I was not ready to admit. Eventually pragmatism won out over pride, and my knees have been grateful ever since.

That's a common conversion story. Trekking poles are one of those pieces of gear where the gap between perceived vanity and actual utility is enormous. Your knees are making the right argument. They'll be especially useful on the Salmon River Trail if any sections are slick — the combination of moss, roots, and damp conditions in late March can make footing unpredictable in spots.

Much appreciated. One last question — any sections of that trail that tend to be muddy this time of year? I have a pair of boots that are, shall we say, approaching their Thermidorian phase. They have served honorably but may not survive a prolonged encounter with deep mud.

Ha — a fine analogy. The trail does have some muddy sections in late March, particularly in the first mile and around any creek crossings. Nothing impassable, but there are spots where you'll want to pick your line carefully. If your boots are truly on their last campaign, gaiters might help extend their tenure, or you could stick to the drier sections and turn around before the trail gets rougher past the four-mile mark. Either way, I'd say the trail is manageable as long as you're not wading through anything.
I will tread carefully, in every sense. Thank you for the thorough recommendations. This was exactly the kind of help I needed.
Happy to help. Enjoy the hike tomorrow, whichever trail you choose. A quiet day in old growth sounds like exactly the right antidote for a long week.
